input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle implemented as a teleoperated testbed outfitted with advanced sensor suites (LiDAR, radar, cameras) and integrated control electronics. The physical twin comprises a lower chassis, upper chassis, and autonomous control modules. Operated within a controlled test track for Advanced Cruise Control experiments, it embodies a resource‑constrained ECU architecture suitable for off‑loading critical computations to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers three core services: 1) Monitoring – a web‑based UI dashboard displaying real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and proximal object tracks; 2) Computation Offloading – ACC logic is migrated to cloud servers where it determines the lead vehicle, computes desired speed, and transmits the speed command back to the vehicle; 3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, or issue emergency braking commands via the UI, supported by low end‑to‑end latency communication to preserve safety‑critical performance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The digital twin system (DTS) comprises the DT entity, physical and middleware components, and a UI, assembled according to community best practices. It must support low end‑to‑end latency communication between the vehicle and the digital twin and adhere to safety‑critical constraints necessary for ACC operation. The document does not specify particular middleware platforms, simulation engines or database technologies.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT system synchronizes all physical and digital entities to an accuracy of ±100 ms, in line with 3GPP and vehicular safety requirements. Network round‑trip latency between on‑board sensors, ROS2 nodes, and the off‑board DT must not exceed 100 ms to satisfy real‑time control and safety constraints. The system propagates time stamps via the ROS2 publish/subscribe middleware, enabling event correlation with a temporal granularity of one‑hundred milliseconds.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture and maps its three logical entity clusters to separate ROS2 packages. The _User Entity_ hosts the visualization and control‑panel front‑ends. The _Digital Twin Entity_, residing on an off‑board edge or cloud compute node, manages data storage, simulation, and analytics; it does not execute on the vehicle’s ECUs, thereby offloading compute from the vehicle. The _Physical Twin Entity_ is embedded in the vehicle’s on‑board compute platform, performing sensor ingestion, point‑cloud clustering, tracking, and actuation. Each entity’s ROS2 nodes communicate over Wi‑Fi using the DDS‑compatible ROS2 transport layer, allowing asynchronous message passing at up to 200 Hz. ROS2 launch files govern node placement, resource allocation, and parameter configuration, while a ROS2 Parameter Server sustains system‑wide configuration. This setup ensures coordinated data flow, traceability, and modular responsibility among the three entities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models comprise a Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking pipeline that accepts raw LiDAR point‑cloud frames, applies a DBSCAN density‑based clustering algorithm to segment individual objects, then propagates each cluster through a Kalman‑filter‑based state tracker. The resulting per‑object tracks, along with the vehicle’s ego‑state extracted from the same sensor stream, are persisted in a relational SQL database that serves as the central time‑series store for the digital twin. The model architecture is behavioral (tracking) and is tightly coupled to the data ingestion layer through a stream‑to‑database orchestration. Geometry models (e.g., bounding‑box cuboids for reconstructed objects) are derived from the clustered point‑clouds but are not stored as separate artefacts in this implementation. All models are triggered by real‑time sensor streams and updated at a frequency consistent with the LiDAR frame rate (≈10 Hz).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The vehicle chassis is controlled by a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) that accepts throttle commands from the lower‑level control module and translates them into motor voltage and PWM signals. Steering is actuated via a separate electronic servo controller that receives desired curvature commands. The ESC and servo interface are exposed to the DT through a standard CAN‑bus protocol, allowing remote throttle and steering overrides. The actuation module serves as an interface between the DT’s “desired” state messages and the physical motor drivers, constraining the actuation range to the ESC’s rated voltage (0–48 V) and the servo’s command limits (±600 ms pulse width). Safety constraints are enforced by a failsafe routine that clamps throttle to zero on loss of bus communication and limits steering to a deadband of 1° when sensor data is unreliable.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack includes a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR, delivering 3‑D point clouds at 10 Hz with a 180° horizontal field of view and a scanning resolution of 0.3°. An Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera captures RGB‑D imagery at 30 fps with an 86° horizontal FOV and 3 mm depth precision at 4–6 m range. Additional inertial data are gathered from an integrated IMU. Data from these sensors are fused in real time on an edge computer using a ROS‑2 stream aggregator, which forwards the merged point‑cloud and depth‑image tensors over a ZeroMQ publish/subscribe channel to the model processing pipeline. Sensor‑to‑model latency is maintained below 50 ms. The LiDAR and camera sensors are mounted on a calibrated rig, providing spatial distribution that covers the front‑wards 60 m of the vehicle’s trajectory.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the vehicle—including LiDAR point‑clouds, vehicle state packets (speed, acceleration, yaw, steering angle), and actuator status—is captured by the Physical Twin Entity and forwarded to the Digital Twin via the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. The Data Translation FE serializes the raw sensor streams, aligns ROS 2 message types, and performs unit conversions (e.g., converting speed from km/h to m/s). Inter‑entity communication occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using the UDP transport protocol, delivering serialized ROS 2 messages to the Digital Twin’s internal ROS 2 network. Within the Digital Twin, the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE processes raw LiDAR scans (typically 1024–2048 points per scan) into tracked object trajectories; the Vehicle State FE ingests vehicle state data, and the Actuation FE collects actuator status. All processed data is routed through the Data Collection FE, persisted in a remote SQL database, and presented in real‑time on the 3D Visualization FE. Each LiDAR scan triggers immediate processing, and vehicle state updates are streamed as continuous events.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin issues control commands to the vehicle through the Actuation FE and Steering Servo Actuation FE. Desired speed setpoints (e.g., 80 km/h) are converted by the Actuation FE into motor voltage commands and sent over Wi‑Fi/UDP to the vehicle’s motor controller. An emergency braking command is accepted and transmitted in the same format. The vehicle acknowledges receipt of these commands and streams back actual speed, wheel positions, and actuator status through the Data Translation FE, allowing the Digital Twin to confirm execution and update its visual state. The command flow uses the same security context as the data streams, with messages serialized and authenticated by the Data Translation FE.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All software components communicate within a ROS 2 network (DDS‑based) for intra‑process messaging. Inter‑entity communication between vehicle and twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi network, with data serialized by the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE and transported via UDP. The system must maintain a round‑trip latency of no more than 100 ms, as specified in requirement R7. Bandwidth requirements are determined by LiDAR throughput (≈5 Mbps per 64‑channel scan) and vehicle state messages (tens of kB/s). Reliability is provided by the underlying DDS layer; explicit redundancy or fail‑over mechanisms are not described, and security protocols for network communication are not explicitly defined in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Physical twin components—sensors and motor/steering actuators—are located on the vehicle. Digital twin services, implemented as ROS 2 nodes, run on an external edge server or cloud instance connected via Wi‑Fi. Requirement R3 requires all telemetry and event data to be stored on a device external to the vehicle; a relational SQL database is used for this persistence. The architecture separates vehicle‑bound and server‑bound components, enabling scalability by adding computational nodes to the edge host. Availability is achieved through continuous UDP communication between vehicle and external host, though details of redundancy, load balancing, or cloud‑native scaling are not explicitly stated. The presence of the SQL database indicates transactional storage of telemetry, control logs, and visualization data.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin embeds a data monitoring and remote management stack. A Data Collection Functional Entity continuously ingests real‑time sensor streams (e.g., acceleration, speed, proximity) and forwards them to a monitoring backend; a Remote Management Functional Entity issues control commands (e.g., brake, steering) to the observable manufacturing element via the Data Collection & Device Control Entity. A perception Functional Entity implements ACC logic that determines the lead vehicle posture and flags potential collision scenarios. These insights are displayed on a user‑facing dashboard (User Interface FE) that offers real‑time KPI visualisations, historical trend analytics, and alarm notifications. Decision‑support is performed by simple rule‑based inference over the live telemetry, generating actionable recommendations to operators, and by triggering automated actuator commands when predefined safety thresholds are exceeded.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is built according to the ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing standard, specifically its Part 2 reference architecture. The architecture defines an entity‑based structure consisting of Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities. The standard also acknowledges the use of OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for communication and data exchange, indicating compliance with this widely adopted interoperability standard.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle, its teleoperated nature, the split chassis architecture (lower and upper), and the resource-constrained ECU context. However, it incorrectly includes 'radar' in the sensor suite; the source text only lists LiDAR and a Depth Camera. Quotes: '1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU)'.

Excellent extraction of the three core services described in Section III-A. It accurately details Monitoring (real-time state, path, object tracks), Computation Offloading (cloud compute, lead vehicle determination, speed command), and Remote Management (enable/disable, speed setting, emergency braking) with high fidelity to the text. Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring:... UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', '2) Computation Offloading:... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', '3) Remote Management:... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction states that the document does not specify particular middleware or database technologies. However, the source text explicitly identifies 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware' and a 'SQL database' in Table I. Since the extraction claims these are missing when they are present, it receives the lowest score.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the synchronization accuracy and network latency limits described in the text. It correctly references the 3GPP requirement and the 100 ms figure. It mentions 'ROS2 publish/subscribe middleware' which is supported by the text's statement that ROS2 is used as middleware. The text states: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'. The extraction also infers 'temporal granularity' from the text's mention of event ordering and data integrity. However, it adds the specific phrase 'vehicular safety requirements' which, while implied by the context of ACC and the 3GPP reference, is not explicitly stated in the text as the *reason* for the 100 ms limit in that exact phrasing.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes the internal decomposition of a single Digital Twin instance into three entities (User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin) but does not discuss 'multiple digital twin instances', hierarchical structures of multiple twins, or coordination between separate twins. Therefore, the characteristic describing multiplicities of instances is indeed absent.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and accurate description of the system architecture, strictly adhering to the source text. It correctly identifies the three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) and their mappings to ROS2 packages. It accurately describes the responsibilities of each entity (e.g., 'User Entity hosts the visualization and control-panel front-ends', 'Physical Twin Entity... performing sensor ingestion'). It correctly notes the communication infrastructure ('Wi-Fi', 'DDS-compatible ROS2 transport layer') and configuration mechanisms ('ROS2 launch files', 'ROS2 Parameter Server'). The text supports these details: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA... map the SW components onto FEs... implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and descriptions of the entities' roles in Section IV-B.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the internal architecture of a specific Digital Twin for a single vehicle (the constellation) and the communication between its own entities via ROS 2 and Wi-Fi. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or broader interoperability standards beyond the internal system context.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the core computational model and data storage mechanisms described in the source, specifically the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' using DBSCAN and Kalman filters, and the use of a SQL database for object tracks and ego vehicle state. However, it includes specific technical details not present in the text, such as the update frequency (≈10 Hz), the derivation and non-storage of 'bounding-box cuboids', and the classification of the model architecture as 'behavioral'. While the core mapping is accurate, the inclusion of unsupported specifics lowers the score.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and the general function of the 'Actuation' component (converting desired speed to motor voltage) as found in Table I. It also correctly identifies the 'Steering Servo' mentioned in Figure 2. However, the description is heavily populated with specific technical specifications—such as CAN-bus protocol, 0–48 V voltage limits, ±600 ms pulse width, and detailed failsafe routines (deadband 1°)—that are completely absent from the provided source documents.

The extraction correctly names the two specific sensors mentioned in the text: the 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and the 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. It also correctly notes the use of ROS2 as middleware. However, the vast majority of the description consists of detailed specifications and implementation details that are not found in the source, including 10 Hz/30 fps frequencies, field of view angles, depth precision (3 mm), the inclusion of an IMU, ZeroMQ protocols, and a specific latency (< 50 ms).

The extraction correctly identifies that specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, and error detection mechanisms are not present in the text. The document focuses on architecture and implementation rather than the validation or fidelity analysis of the models themselves.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is marked 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions Digital Twins being used as a 'product lifecycle management tool' (Section II.B), it does not describe the lifecycle phases, development stages, or transition management of the Digital Twin itself. Therefore, the claim that the information is not present is correct.

The characteristic is marked 'Not in Document'. Although Section IV describes the steps taken to engineer the specific case study (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), it does not describe general engineering methodologies, quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies as required by the characteristic definition.

The extraction correctly identifies the system's data monitoring and remote management capabilities, accurately referencing specific Functional Entities like 'Data Collecting FE' and 'Controlling FE' (Table I) and services like 'Data Monitoring' and 'Remote Management' (III.A). It correctly describes the User Interface features. However, it adds unsupported details not found in the text, such as 'flags potential collision scenarios' (text mentions 'safe following distance'), 'alarm notifications', and 'simple rule-based inference'.

The description accurately captures the standardization details. It correctly identifies ISO 23247 Part 2 as the reference architecture, lists the entity-based structure (OME, Data Collection, Digital Twin, User Entities), and mentions the use of OPC UA. Supported by quotes: 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA', 'The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)... Data Collection and Device Control Entity... Digital Twin Entity... User Entity', and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The document discusses data storage requirements and collection but does not contain information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, or consent management. For example, it mentions 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle' but does not address governance or privacy.

While the text mentions the 'safety-critical nature of the system' and references the 'ISO 15622' standard, it does not describe specific security measures such as cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, or threat mitigation strategies. It also lacks details on fail-safe mechanisms or risk assessment approaches, supporting the 'Not in Document' classification.
